coredata.dc:title: Tribological properties of graphene quantum dot hybrid polyethylene glycol lubricated molybdenum disulfide films
coredata.dc:creator: Ao, Dong
Fan, Xiaoqiang
Zeng, Zihan
Zhu, Minhao
Ye, Xiangyuan
Shang, Lunlin
coredata.dc:description: Herein, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) films were obtained by magnetron sputtering, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) prepared by the hydrothermal method were used as additives for polyethylene glycol (PEG) lubricants to investigate the tribological mechanisms under solid-liquid composite lubrication. The friction coefficient of GQDs hybridized PEG lubricated MoS2 solid-liquid composite films was reduced by 69.23%, and the wear rate was reduced by 79.31% as compared to solid lubrication (MoS2 film). This solid-liquid complex lubrication occurs mainly as abrasive wear. GQDs form a uniform and dense amorphous carbon film on the corresponding balls to protect the wear between the friction pair. This work demonstrates the great potential of zero-dimensional and two-dimensional materials for solid-liquid composite lubrication.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molybdenum disulfide films
Graphene quantum dots
Polyethylene glycol
Solid-liquid composite lubrication

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs hybrid PEG dispersion
Tribological properties of GQDs hybrid PEG lubricated MoS2 films
Morphology and composition analyses of wear surfaces and wear debris

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52075458 and U2141211), and Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest Jiaotong University for providing testing services.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The morphology and composition of MoS2: (a) SEM, (b)AFM, (c)Raman, (d)XRD, (e, f) XPS diagram of MoS2 films.
Variation of friction coefficient at (a) 2 N, (b) 5 N, (c) 20 N and (d) mean friction coefficient.
Two-dimensional morphology of wear tacks at (a) 2 N, (b) 5 N, (c) 20 N and (d) wear rate.
Optical microscopic analysis of wear tracks at (a - c) 2 N, (d - f) 5 N, (g - i) 20 N.
SEM analysis of wear tracks at (a)∼(c) 2 N, (d)∼(f) 5 N, (g)∼(i) 20 N.
Wear pattern morphology and element analysis of counterpart ball at 5 N.
SEM and EDS analysis of wear debris at 5 N.
Raman analysis of (a) wear surface, (b) the counterpart ball surface and (c) XRD analysis of wear surface at 5 N.
Raman analysis of counterpart balls at 5 N: (a)∼ (c) single solid lubrication (MoS2 films), (d)∼(f) solid-liquid composite lubrication (MoS2 +PEG+GQDs).
XPS analysis of the wear tracks at 5 N.
Lubrication mechanism of MoS2 films single lubrication and GQDs hybrid PEG lubricated MoS2 films.

head.ce:title: Tribological properties of graphene quantum dot hybrid polyethylene glycol lubricated molybdenum disulfide films

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) films were obtained by magnetron sputtering, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) prepared by the hydrothermal method were used as additives for polyethylene glycol (PEG) lubricants to investigate the tribological mechanisms under solid-liquid composite lubrication. The friction coefficient of GQDs hybridized PEG lubricated MoS2 solid-liquid composite films was reduced by 69.23%, and the wear rate was reduced by 79.31% as compared to solid lubrication (MoS2 film). This solid-liquid complex lubrication occurs mainly as abrasive wear. GQDs form a uniform and dense amorphous carbon film on the corresponding balls to protect the wear between the friction pair. This work demonstrates the great potential of zero-dimensional and two-dimensional materials for solid-liquid composite lubrication.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Friction is commonplace in our daily lives. Ineffective control of wear and tear is usually a major factor in shortening the life and decreasing the reliability of machinery and equipment. One of the effective ways to control friction is to use lubricants in liquid or solid form [1–4]. According to the physical state of the lubricant can be divided into two categories: solid lubricants and liquid lubricants. Solid lubricants (powders, films, and coatings) have good adhesion to friction surfaces and can serve to lower the coefficient of friction and reduce wear. At the same time, they are suitable for a wide temperature range and have a high bearing capacity. So solid lubricants are often used in extreme environmental conditions to minimize frictional energy dissipation and wear damage [5,6]. However, there are also disadvantages such as poor thermal conductivity and easy to produce debris. In contrast, liquid lubricants have a lower coefficient of friction and a longer service life [7]. Unfortunately, liquid lubricants are prone to volatilization and degradation at high temperatures. Therefore, solid-liquid composite lubrication has been proposed, which can make up for the lack of lubrication of the two alone, while meeting lower friction and wider adaptability, and improving the service life of mechanical parts [8–13].

Utilizing two-dimensional structural materials as lubricants to achieve efficient lubrication has become a research hotspot [14,15]. Among them, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), as a typical two-dimensional layered structure material, is prone to sliding between atomic layers when subjected to tangential forces, which can realize ultra-low friction [16–20]. Li et al. [16] used a combination of in situ scanning electron microscopy techniques and friction force sensors for silicon nanowire cantilevers to perform friction tests on sliding between disproportionate MoS2 monolayers. The coefficient of friction is about 10-4 under super-lubrication. Gao et al. [21] explored the application of MoS2-Au/Au multilayers for aerospace applications, which continued to exhibit good lubrication (coefficient of friction of about 0.02–0.05) under space-exposed conditions. Meanwhile, there are also related explorations in the solid-liquid composite lubrication with MoS2 film and some lubricants. Fan et al. [2] explored the lubrication performance of gear oils compounded with MoS2 film, which improved the wear reduction and wear resistance by 46.7% and 97.1%, respectively, compared to lubrication with a single molybdenum disulfide film. Hou et al. [22] explored the synergistic friction of synthetic oil poly-alpha-olefins (PAO) with MoS2 coating, the coefficient of friction was only a quarter of that of the solid molybdenum disulfide film. The composite lubrication provided better friction reduction and wear resistance, the film load carrying capacity was increased by 6 times, and the service life was greatly extended.

GQDs are the embodiment of graphene (a two-dimensional material) on the zero-dimensional scale, and have also achieved good applications as lubricant additives in friction reduction and anti-wear [23–27]. By investigating the synergistic effect of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with glycerol aqueous solution, M. Kalin et al. [28] demonstrated that green nano-lubricants based on GQDs can provide ultra-low friction at high contact pressures in the boundary lubrication zone. He et al. [29] synthesized GQDs with good oil solubility with 150SN mineral oil by the gas detonation method. Due to the synergistic effect of polishing and repairing, GQDs have good antifriction performance and excellent anti-wear performance.

In MoS2 film solid-liquid composite lubrication system, the selection of liquid lubricant is of great significance to obtain the composite lubrication and achieve low wear. Compared with other traditional lubricants, polyethylene glycol (PEG) is a non-toxic polymer composed of repeating glycol units that has a wide range of applications as an environmentally friendly material, such as non-ionic surfactants, lubricants, solvents, and so on [30–32]. Meanwhile, good dispersion of GQDs in PEG had been demonstrated in previous studies, and Wang et al. also found that the introduction of GQDs into PEG aqueous solution could shorten the running-in period [33,34]. Therefore, it is important to explore the solid-liquid composite lubrication role of zero-dimensional materials (GQDs) as PEG lubricant additives with two-dimensional layered material MoS2 films.

In this paper, the synergistic friction process of MoS2 film and PEG under different loads was investigated, and the effect of GQDs lubricating oil additive on solid-liquid composite lubrication was compared. The lubrication mechanism of two-dimensional material MoS2 and zero-dimensional material GQDs under the effect of different forms of composite has been explored through a variety of means of characterization, which is of some significance for solid-liquid composite lubrication.

PEG, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and absolute ethanol were obtained from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co Ltd, and graphene oxide (GO) was purchased from Suzhou Tanfeng Technology Co Ltd. Deionized water was used for the experimental procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs hybrid PEG dispersion
ce:section.ce:para: 5 mg GO with few layers was uniformly dispersed in 15 mL absolute ethanol by sonication for 2 h to obtain a gray-black suspension. Subsequently, 15 mL H2O2 was added and reacted in a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave at 180 °C for 2 h to produce a transparent yellow liquid. After that, the above solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane, and subjected to vacuum rotary steaming and freeze-drying to obtain colloidal GQDs. In addition, detailed preparation methods and characterization of GQDs can be found in our previous studies [33]. In the end, GQDs were mixed in PEG at a content of 0.04 wt% to complete a lubricant containing the GQDs additive.

A Teer UDP-650 closed-field magnetron sputtering system was used to deposit MoS2 films on AISI 304 stainless steel substrates [35]. Prior to deposition, the stainless steel substrates underwent ultrasonic cleaning with acetone and alcohol for 20 min to eliminate surface stains from the samples. Then the substrates were dried with hot air. Afterwards, the substrates were subjected to a vacuum environment with argon gas, and a bias pressure of − 500 V was applied to eliminate surface contamination from the substrate. MoS2 was provided using two 550 W targets, while the transition layer of titanium (Ti) was deposited using two 150 W targets [36]. The generation of the MoS2 film was initiated upon reducing the DC bias voltage from 150 V to 50 V.

The surface morphology of the MoS2 films was analyzed by a tungsten filament scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-IT500). The atomic force microscope (AFM, FM-Nanoview 1000) was used to measure the microscopic surface morphology and roughness of the MoS2 films. The crystal structure of the MoS2 films was characterized by diffraction (XRD, Empyrean, PANalytical) at 2θ angles of 20˚ to 80˚ at 40 kV and 40 mA current. Raman spectra of the MoS2 films were obtained using a Raman spectrometer (Horiba LabRam HR800) under 532 nm laser excitation. To determine the composition of the MoS2 films and the bonding states of the relevant elements, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy measurements (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi) were carried out on them.

To systematically compare the tribological properties under solid lubrication and solid-liquid composite lubrication, the friction experiments were carried out using a reciprocating friction machine under three sets of loads: 2 N, 5 N, and 20 N (the maximum Hertz contact stress calculated as 592.2 MPa, 803.7 MPa, and 1275.8 MPa, respectively). The tribological behaviors of the above tests were evaluated under the displacement amplitude of 0.5 mm, and frequency of 5 Hz over duration of 2000 s, and at least three replicate tests were carried out, respectively. The steel balls (with a diameter of 9.525 mm) were selected for the upper sample, which were made of GCr15 steel (AISI 52100). The corresponding film substrates (AISI 304 stainless steel) were held by a fixture. 3D images of the wear tracks formed on the film surface after the friction experiments were recorded using a white light interferometer (WLI, Contour GT) to obtain two-dimensional cross-sectional curves of the wear marks. An optical microscope (motic BA310Met) and a tungsten filament scanning electron microscope (JSM-IT500) were used to analyze the wear tracks and the wear scars morphology and elemental content on the counterpart spheres. The wear debris after friction was collected and analyzed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JSM 7800 F) to observe the morphology and elemental distribution. The Raman spectra of the wear surface were measured at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm, as well as the Mo and C elemental distribution. The crystal structure of the wear marks was characterized by diffraction (XRD, Empyrean, PANalytical) at a 2θ angle of 20˚ to 80˚ at 40 kV and 40 mA current. Eventually, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi) was used to analyze the composition of the abrasives and the bonding states of the associated elements.

The morphology and composition of MoS2 films are shown in Fig. 1. The SEM and AFM images of the surface of the MoS2 films are shown in Fig. 1(a) and Fig. 1(b), respectively, and show that the films have a uniform and dense surface with a roughness of 2.87 nm and a good surface profile. In the Raman spectrum of the MoS2 films as in Fig. 1(c), the characteristic peaks at around 380 cm-1 and 410 cm-1 are
E
2 g
1
and A1 g, respectively, which are caused by two S and Mo atoms vibrating in opposite directions in the same plane, reflecting their interlayer structural information [37–39]. Fig. 1(d) shows XRD analysis of MoS2 films, with peaks around 38.6°, 53.3°, and 76.9° corresponding to the (1 0 3), (0 1 8), and (1 0 1 3) crystal planes of MoS2. XPS analysis was performed on the films, and in the XPS pattern of Mo 3d (Fig. 1(e)), the two characteristic peaks around 229.0 eV and 232.2 eV correspond to Mo4+ (3d5/2) and Mo4+ (3d3/2) of the molybdenum element in MoS2, and the two characteristic peaks around 232.5 eV and 235.4 eV correspond to Mo6+ in MoO3 [40]. Meanwhile, as can be seen from the XPS pattern of S 2p (Fig. 1(f)), the two characteristic peaks near 161.8 eV and 163.0 eV correspond to S2- (2p3/2) and S2- (2p1/2) of the elemental sulfur in MoS2, respectively. It demonstrates that the film composition is predominantly MoS2 with a small amount of MoO3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological properties of GQDs hybrid PEG lubricated MoS2 films
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the tribological performance of GQDs hybrid PEG lubricated MoS2 films, the coefficients of friction were investigated under different lubricating media of 2 N, 5 N, and 20 N. From Fig. 2, it is observed that under solid lubrication, the friction coefficient tends to decrease as the load increases from 2 N to 20 N. This indicates that the MoS2 films exhibits lower friction coefficients at higher loads, but the friction coefficient fluctuates widely and the friction process is unstable, which is a common drawback of solid lubrication [41]. Solid-liquid composite lubrication, compared to solid lubrication, significantly reduces the friction coefficients. The friction behavior attains steady-state in a brief duration, and the overall friction coefficient is more stable. This is due to the liquid lubrication possessing high fluidity, which facilitates smooth friction behavior, and the oil film exhibiting some resilience and ductility. Compared with solid lubrication (MoS2 films), the coefficient of friction of solid-liquid composite lubrication with GQDs hybrid PEG was reduced by 71.50%, 69.23%, and 40.28% at 2 N, 5 N, and 20 N, respectively. At the same time, the coefficient of friction was reduced by 20.52% with the addition of GQDs at 5 N compared to pure PEG solid-liquid lubrication. Therefore, GQDs hybrid PEG lubricated MoS2 films are effective in reducing the coefficient of friction.

In order to further analyze the wear under different lubrication media, white light interference analysis was performed on the wear tracks after the friction experiment, and the two-dimensional profile shape in Fig. 3 was obtained. It can be observed that the depth of the wear tracks increases significantly with the load increment under solid lubrication medium, reflecting that increased loading leads to more wear on the MoS2 films surface. However, the change of friction load under a solid-liquid composite lubrication medium has less effect on the depth of the wear tracks, which is consistent with the change of friction coefficient. This indicates that the oil film during friction has good strength and toughness to carry the increased load, thus the friction coefficient under solid-liquid compound lubrication shows better stability while the wear depth and wear volume changes are smaller compared to solid lubrication. Based on the depth of the wear marks, Fig. 3(d) shows the wear rate of different lubricating media under three types of loads. At a load of 5 N, the solid-liquid composite lubrication with pure PEG lubricant reduced the wear rate by 37.93% compared to solid lubrication. The solid-liquid composite system with the addition of GQDs additive reduced the wear rate by 79.31% compared to solid lubrication, and the GQDs hybrid PEG reduced the wear rate by 66.67% compared to the solid-liquid composite lubrication with pure PEG. This indicates that the addition of liquid lubricant can effectively reduce the wear rate of the film surface, and the addition of GQDs can improve the wear resistance of the solid-liquid composite, and GQDs play a protective role on the film surface, which greatly reduces the wear rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and composition analyses of wear surfaces and wear debris
ce:section.ce:para: Analysis of the overall wear morphology of wear surfaces is essential to studying the tribological properties of GQDs hybrid PEG lubricated MoS2 films. As shown in Fig. 4, optical microscopy analysis was carried out on the friction tracks formed at different lubrication media under three different loads. As the load increases, the wear marks are deeper and wider after solid lubrication (Fig. 4(a, d)). When the load is 20 N (Fig. 4(g)), the surface of the solid lubricated friction shows a large area of spalling, indicating that serious adhesive wear occurs on the film surface during the friction process. This corresponds to the two-dimensional profile of the wear tracks, where the wear tracks are deeper and serious spalling occurs on the film surface. Under solid-liquid composite lubrication conditions with pure PEG and MoS2 films, the wear on the film surface mainly originates from abrasive wear, and the film wear becomes more severe with increasing load, with more grooves appearing (Fig. 4(b, e, h)). However, with the addition of GQDs to the PEG lubricant, the abrasive wear under all three loads was reduced, and no significant adhesive wear was observed under the solid-liquid composite lubrication (Fig. 4(c, f, i)). Hence, the addition of GQDs helped to reduce the formation of surface furrows, which reduced the abrasive wear under pure PEG lubricant, and improved the wear of the film surface during the friction process, and played a good role in protecting the MoS2 films.

Meanwhile, in order to observe the surface morphology of the wear track more clearly, their SEM characterization under different working conditions is shown in Fig. 5. Abrasive wear is predominant under solid-liquid composite lubrication (MoS2 +PEG), and the addition of liquid lubricant can inhibit the spalling on the film surface, which has a certain protective effect on the film. With the increase in load, the width of wear tracks after different lubrication media showed different degrees of increase. The width of wear tracks under solid lubrication increased most significantly, and the width of wear tracks after GQDs hybrid PEG solid-liquid composite lubrication was less affected by the increase in load, indicating that GQDs as additives help to improve the lubrication performance of the oil film. The addition of GQDs to the solid-liquid composite lubrication reduced the wear track width by 25.11%, 38.67%, and 26.55%, respectively at 2 N, 5 N, and 20 N, compared to solid lubrication alone. The wear track width of solid-liquid composite lubrication with GQDs was also lower by 14.02%, 10.14%, and 2.07%, respectively, than that of solid-liquid composite lubrication without GQDs. Consequently, this shows that GQDs hybrid PEG lubricated MoS2 films can effectively reduce the wear on the friction surfaces compared to solid lubrication.

Based on the above analysis, the solid-liquid composite lubrication is more effective in reducing the coefficient of friction and improving the surface wear at the load of 5 N. In order to further analyze the effect of different lubrication media on the wear surface, SEM morphology analysis and EDS elemental analysis were performed on the wear scars on the counterpart ball at 5 N ( Fig. 6). Under solid lubrication conditions, the diameter of the wear scar on the counterpart ball was 181.81 µm, and the surface showed significant spalling and adhesive wear. EDS analysis showed the presence of thin film characteristic elements Mo and S on the surface of the counterpart ball, indicating the formation of a friction transfer film during the wear process. The transfer film formed between the friction pairs can prevent direct contact between the friction surfaces, which can provide some protection to the MoS2 films. However, according to the distribution of Mo and S elements, it can be observed that the formation of transfer film distribution is not uniform and not dense. It is more distributed at the edge of the grinding spot, with more serious oxidation occurring in the center of the grinding spot. Under the solid-liquid complex lubrication condition, the diameters of wear scars were reduced by 22.52% (PEG+MoS2) and 73.01% (PEG+GQDs+MoS2), respectively, and no Mo or S elements were observed in any of them, which indicates that the solid-liquid complex lubrication attenuates the wear and inhibits the transfer of the MoS2 film. The solid-liquid composite lubrication with GQDs can reduce oxidation on the surface of the counterpart ball. The surface C element content increases and O element content decreases, probably because GQDs promote the generation of oil film and participate in the formation of lubricating film. They are adsorbed on the surface of the counterpart ball to increase the C content of the wear scar, thus better protecting the counterpart ball and film and reducing the surface wear rate. This is consistent with the results of the analysis in Fig. 3.

SEM and EDS characterization were performed on the wear debris generated by different lubricating media during the friction process at 5 N, and their morphology and elements were analyzed as shown in Fig. 7. The wear debris generated under solid lubrication has a larger size and thicker thickness, reflecting severe wear on the surface of the friction pairs at this time. This is consistent with the analysis results in Figs. 5 and 6, where there are deep furrows and peeling on the surface. Under solid-liquid composite lubrication, the size of wear debris decreases. Based on the analysis of wear track morphology in Figs. 4 and 5, the addition of lubricating oil results in the formation of fewer and shallower furrows on wear surfaces as the small-sized wear debris participates in friction. Compared with solid lubrication, it improves wear on the surface of MoS2 film. The surface of wear debris under solid lubrication has a high content of Mo, S, and O elements, indicating that a large number of MoS2 films participate in friction reactions and partially adhere to the wear debris. This is also confirmed by the MoS2 transfer film formed on the counterpart ball in Fig. 6(a-c). The surface of the film is peeled; part of it is transferred to the wear debris, and the other part is transferred to the surface of the counterpart ball. Nevertheless, under the condition of solid-liquid compound lubrication (GQDs+PEG+MoS2), the content of O element on the wear debris surface decreases, which indicates that oil lubrication can reduce the oxidation in the friction process. The low content of Mo and S elements confirmed the protective effect of lubricating oil on MoS2 films, restrained the transfer of thin film material, and reduced the wear rate of friction pair material.

It is crucial to investigate the formation of lubrication films and the mechanism of solid-liquid complex interaction under different lubrication media. As shown in Fig. 8, the wear surface and wear scars were analyzed by Raman spectroscopy at 5 N, and the wear surfaces were analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 8(a, b), under the solid-liquid complex lubrication condition with GQDs additives, no graphene signature peaks appeared on the surface of the wear marks, indicating that no GQDs were deposited on the surface of the film, whereas graphene signature peaks appeared on the surface of the counterpart ball (D peaks at ca. 1340 cm-1 and G peaks at ca. 1580 cm-1), suggesting that the GQDs had been deposited on the surface of the counterpart ball by this time [39]. Due to the frictional chemical reaction on the surface of the counterpart sphere (GCr15), the electrons escape and become positively charged. The GQDs in the liquid lubricant contain hydroxyl groups that are negatively charged, and due to electrostatic adsorption, they are easily attached to the surface of the counterpart ball to form a lubricating film to protect the tribo-pairs, while no electrostatic adsorption occurs on the surface of the MoS2 films [42]. During solid lubrication, the characteristic peak of MoS2 appears on the counterpart ball, and the film is transferred, forming a MoS2 transfer film on the counterpart ball. This is in agreement with the EDS analysis in Fig. 6. From the analysis of Fig. 8(c), it can be seen that the addition of PEG and GQDs do not change the crystal structure and lattice spacing of MoS2 in the abrasion marks of a single solid film after friction. Comparing with Fig. 1(d), it can be seen that the friction process has little effect on the crystal structure and lattice spacing of MoS2 films.

To confirm the formation of the lubrication film, Raman analysis was performed on the counterpart ball following solid lubrication at 5 N and solid-liquid composite lubrication with GQDs, as shown in Fig. 9. The Raman spectra of different regions show variations in color, reflecting the difference in the Raman peak intensities of the constituent substances. The color scale ranges from red to green to blue, corresponding to high, medium, and low intensities, respectively. Fig. 9(a) shows the Raman photomicrographs of the counterpart ball after solid lubrication. The presence of the transfer film can be clearly observed, but its distribution is non-uniform, as shown by Raman mapping (Fig. 9(b)) and point analysis (Fig. 9(c)). There are relatively few red areas, and the green and blue areas lack the characteristic MoS2 peaks from Raman analysis, indicating that the transfer film is sparse. Fig. 9(d) shows the Raman photomicrographs of the counterpart ball after solid-liquid composite lubrication. A large area of red regions, indicating high carbon peak intensities, can be observed in Raman surface mapping (Fig. 9(e)) and point analysis (Fig. 9(f)). The green areas also show the characteristic peaks of graphene, indicating that a more uniform and dense carbon film is formed on the surface of the counterpart ball. Meanwhile, the intensity of the D peak is higher than that of the G peak, indicating that the film is composed of highly defective amorphous carbon [33,43]. Therefore, compared to the single solid lubrication, the solid-liquid composite lubrication with GQDs formed a more uniform and dense film, which could better protect the surface of the counterpart ball.

In order to investigate the chemical reactions occurring in the wear tracks during the friction process, XPS analysis was performed on the abrasion marks under different lubrication media, as shown in Fig. 10. Compared with the initial MoS2 films, the film composition after solid lubrication and solid-liquid composite lubrication still consisted of MoS2 and MoO3. Meanwhile, the comparison and analysis of the Mo6+ peak area revealed that the content of MoO3 in the wear marks after solid-liquid composite lubrication was lower than that after solid lubrication. This is attributed to the addition of lubricants that create an oil-lubricated environment for friction. It isolates the air and forms an oil film that protects the wear surface from contacting the air and undergoing oxidation reactions. This is consistent with the results of the wear debris analysis in Fig. 7. The content of MoO3 in the wear marks after the addition of GQDs to the solid-liquid composite lubrication was also lower than that after pure PEG. The analysis combined with Fig. 9 shows that GQDs produce a dense and uniform lubricating film that adheres to the counterpart ball during the friction process, preventing direct contact between the counterpart ball and the MoS2 films. Hence, the addition of GQDs helps to reduce oxidation on the wear surface.

The results of the above analysis were combined to propose a reasonable solid lubrication and solid-liquid composite lubrication mechanism. Fig. 11 shows a schematic diagram of friction under solid lubrication and solid-liquid composite lubrication containing GQDs additives. The friction experiment utilizes a point-surface contact mode, with the load acting vertically on a fixed bearing steel ball and the steel block doing reciprocating motion. In solid lubrication, friction occurs between the counterpart ball and the MoS2 films. The MoS2 films are subjected to shear force relative sliding between the layers. When the load is large, the film flakes off macroscopically, while the shedding of material on the counterpart ball also acts as wear debris, further participating in the friction reaction. The wear surface appears deeper, and the wear mechanism manifests as adhesive wear and abrasive wear. The MoS2 films are partially transferred to the counterpart ball during the reciprocal friction process, forming a transfer film. And part of the film is also transferred to the wear debris. In solid-liquid composite lubrication, friction occurs mainly between the counterpart ball and the oil film. The oil film bears part of the shear force and pressure to protect the counterpart ball and MoS2 films, with only a small part of the MoS2 films bearing the force of friction. During the friction process, fewer and smaller sized abrasive chips shed from the counterpart balls are repeatedly involved in the reaction between the friction pair, which prevents direct contact between the friction pair. Shallow furrows are formed on the surface of the film, and the wear mechanism manifests itself as abrasive wear. At the same time, the surface of the counterpart balls is positively charged due to electron escape and electrostatically adsorbs with the negatively charged GQDs in the lubricating oil to form a dense and uniform amorphous carbon film. GQDs are added to improve the strength and toughness of the oil film formed by PEG, which can better carry the role of friction and provide good protection to both the MoS2 films and the counterpart balls.

This study investigates the lubrication behavior of PEG base oils with GQDs as lubricant additives on solid MoS2 films. It compares the tribological performance of friction pairs under different loads and different lubricating media (solid lubrication and solid-liquid composite lubrication), and fully explores the mechanisms of solid-liquid composite lubrication (PEG+GQDs+MoS2). In summary, the following are presented:

(a) The solid-liquid composite lubrication (PEG+GQDs+MoS2) reduced the coefficient of friction by 69.23%, as compared to the solid lubrication (MoS2 films). GQDs hybrid PEG lubricated MoS2 films exhibited excellent anti-friction properties.

(b) The wear rate of GQDs hybrid PEG lubricated MoS2 films was reduced by 79.31% compared to single solid lubrication (MoS2 films) and 66.67% compared to pure PEG solid-liquid composite lubrication. GQDs can enhance the wear resistance of PEG in solid-liquid composites.

(c) Due to the synergistic effect of the oil film with easily-shearing GQDs and MoS2 film, the friction mechanism of the GQDs hybrid PEG lubricated MoS2 films manifests itself as abrasive wear. An amorphous carbon film with uniform density and high degree of defects is formed on the corresponding balls, and the oxidation of the wear surface is reduced.

Xiaoqiang Fan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Zihan Zeng: Visualization. Minhao Zhu: Supervision. Xiangyuan Ye: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Lunlin Shang: Visualization. Dong Ao: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52075458 and U2141211), and Analytical and Testing Center of Southwest Jiaotong University for providing testing services.
